subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 15! In this unit you will look at the world in a new way. You will discover symmetry – the special balance you can see in a butterfly's wings, in the pattern on a masjid door, and in the shape of your own face. You will learn about position – the words we use to say exactly where something is, like left, right, above, below, in front and behind. And you will explore movement – how things turn and travel, using words such as clockwise, anticlockwise, half turn and quarter turn.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Understand what a line of symmetry is and find it in shapes and pictures.
Complete the other half of a symmetrical shape or pattern.
Understand reflection and how a mirror line makes a matching image.
Make your own symmetrical patterns using colours and shapes.
Use position and direction words: left, right, forwards, backwards, above, below, in front of, behind, next to, between.
Describe movement using the words clockwise and anticlockwise.
Describe turns as a half turn or a quarter turn.
Give and follow clear instructions to move from one place to another.

concepts.title: What Is Symmetry?
concepts.explanation: Symmetry is a special kind of balance. A shape or picture has symmetry when you can fold it so that both halves match exactly, with no part sticking out and no part missing. The fold line is called the line of symmetry. When one half sits perfectly on top of the other half, we say the shape is symmetrical. Here is the easiest way to feel what symmetry means. Take a piece of paper and fold it in half. Now imagine painting one half of a shape right up against the fold. If you press the two halves together, the paint…
concepts.example: Look at your own body in a mirror. Your two eyes match, one on each side of your nose. Your two ears match. Your two hands match. There is an invisible line of symmetry running straight down the middle of your face and…

concepts.title: Finding the Line of Symmetry
concepts.explanation: To find a line of symmetry, you look for the fold line that makes the two halves match. There are two easy ways to test it: the folding test and the mirror test. The folding test works like this. Imagine folding the shape along a line. If the two halves land exactly on top of each other, with every edge and corner matching, then that fold line is a line of symmetry. If any part pokes out or does not match, then it is not a line of symmetry. You can really do this with paper shapes – cut out a shape, try to fold…
concepts.example: The mirror test works like this. Stand a small mirror upright along the line you are testing. Look into the mirror. If the reflection you see in the mirror looks exactly like the half of the shape that is hidden behind…

concepts.title: Reflection and the Mirror Line
concepts.explanation: Reflection is what you see when you look in a mirror. The mirror makes a copy of you – but the copy is flipped. When you raise your right hand, your reflection seems to raise the hand on the opposite side. This flipping is the key idea of reflection. In maths, a reflection happens across a mirror line. Every point on one side of the mirror line has a matching point on the other side, exactly the same distance away. If a dot is 3 squares to the left of the mirror line, its reflection is 3 squares to the right. If a…
concepts.example: Here is a clear example. Imagine the letter b held up to a mirror on its right side. In the reflection it looks like the letter d – it has flipped over. The two together, b and its reflection d, are symmetrical about…

concepts.title: Making Symmetrical Patterns
concepts.explanation: Now that you can spot symmetry, you can make it yourself. Symmetrical patterns are used all over the world to decorate carpets, doors, books and clothes. Islamic art is famous for its beautiful, careful patterns, many of which are perfectly symmetrical. When you design your own, you are joining a long and lovely tradition. The simplest way to make a symmetrical pattern is to choose a mirror line and then copy everything you do on one side onto the other side. If you colour a square red on the left, colour the…
concepts.example: You can use colours to make patterns as well. Take a grid and draw a horizontal line of symmetry across the middle. Now colour some squares above the line, then colour the matching squares below the line in the same…

concepts.title: Position and Direction Words
concepts.explanation: Position words tell us exactly where something is. Direction words tell us which way something faces or moves. These words are some of the most useful in the whole of maths, because we use them to give directions and describe the world. Here are the most important ones: Left and right – the two sides. Your right hand is the one you usually write or eat with.
concepts.example: Forwards and backwards – towards where you face, and away behind you.

concepts.title: Turns - Clockwise and Anticlockwise
concepts.explanation: When something spins or turns round, it goes in one of two directions. We name these directions using a clock. Clockwise is the direction the hands of a clock move: from the top, they go to the right, then down, then to the left, then back up. Anticlockwise is the opposite direction: up, then left, then down, then right. The word anticlockwise means against the clock. Picture a clock face on the wall. The hands start at the top (12), move to the 3 on the right, down to the 6 at the bottom, across to the 9 on the…
concepts.example: You can turn your own body clockwise or anticlockwise. Stand up and face forwards. To turn clockwise, turn towards your right shoulder. To turn anticlockwise, turn towards your left shoulder. Try it slowly. Many games…

methods.title: How to find a line of symmetry
methods.example: Work through these with me. Cover the solution, try it yourself first, then check every step.
steps: Imagine folding the shape along a line.
Check whether the two halves land exactly on top of each other.
If they match, it is a line of symmetry. If not, try a different line.
Use a mirror on the line to check: the reflection should match the hidden half.

methods.title: How to complete a reflection
methods.example: Work through these with me. Cover the solution, try it yourself first, then check every step.
steps: Find the mirror line and which way it lies (vertical or horizontal).
For each point, count how many squares it is from the mirror line.
Mark the matching point the same distance on the other side.
Join the points to draw the reflected half.

methods.title: How to work out a turn
methods.example: Hassan has the left half of a shape. A dot sits 3 squares to the left of the vertical mirror line. Where does its reflection go?
steps: Note which way you start facing.
Decide the direction: clockwise (right) or anticlockwise (left).
Count the turn: a quarter turn faces you sideways, a half turn faces you the opposite way.
Say which way you end up facing.

methods.title: Position words
methods.example: Yusuf stands in the courtyard. The well is in front of him and Omar is on his right. Yusuf makes a half turn. Now where is the well, and where is Omar?
steps: Solution: A half turn makes Yusuf face the opposite way.
The well, which was in front, is now behind him.
Omar, who was on his right, is now on his left, because turning right round swaps left and right.
Worked Example 5: Which turn direction?

methods.title: Which turn direction?
methods.example: Yusuf stands in the courtyard. The well is in front of him and Omar is on his right. Yusuf makes a half turn. Now where is the well, and where is Omar?
steps: Solution: A half turn makes Yusuf face the opposite way.
The well, which was in front, is now behind him.
Omar, who was on his right, is now on his left, because turning right round swaps left and right.
Worked Example 5: Which turn direction?

methods.title: Counting quarter turns
methods.example: Halima faces the masjid. She makes two quarter turns clockwise. Which way does she face now?
steps: Solution: One quarter turn clockwise makes her face to her right.
A second quarter turn (two in total) makes another right-corner turn, so she now faces away from the masjid – the opposite direction.
Two quarter turns together make one half turn.

workedExamples.title: Is it symmetrical?
workedExamples.prompt: Work through these with me. Cover the solution, try it yourself first, then check every step.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: Is it symmetrical? Amina looks at a heart shape. Does it have a line of symmetry? Where is it? Solution: Imagine folding the heart down the middle from the top dip to the bottom point. The left half lands exactly on the right half, so yes, the heart is symmetrical. Its line of symmetry is a vertical line down the middle. If you stood a mirror on that line, the reflection would look just like the hidden half. Worked Example 2: How many lines of symmetry? How many lines of symmetry does a square…

workedExamples.title: How many lines of symmetry?
workedExamples.prompt: Work through these with me. Cover the solution, try it yourself first, then check every step.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: Is it symmetrical? Amina looks at a heart shape. Does it have a line of symmetry? Where is it? Solution: Imagine folding the heart down the middle from the top dip to the bottom point. The left half lands exactly on the right half, so yes, the heart is symmetrical. Its line of symmetry is a vertical line down the middle. If you stood a mirror on that line, the reflection would look just like the hidden half. Worked Example 2: How many lines of symmetry? How many lines of symmetry does a square…

workedExamples.title: Complete the reflection
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan has the left half of a shape. A dot sits 3 squares to the left of the vertical mirror line. Where does its reflection go?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A reflection is the same distance on the other side of the mirror line. The dot is 3 squares to the left, so its reflection is 3 squares to the right of the mirror line, at the same height. Count the squares carefully and mark the matching dot.

workedExamples.title: Position words
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf stands in the courtyard. The well is in front of him and Omar is on his right. Yusuf makes a half turn. Now where is the well, and where is Omar?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A half turn makes Yusuf face the opposite way. The well, which was in front, is now behind him. Omar, who was on his right, is now on his left, because turning right round swaps left and right. Worked Example 5: Which turn direction? A clock hand moves from the top (12) round to the 3 on the right. Is this clockwise or anticlockwise? Solution: Clock hands naturally move from 12 towards the 3 on the right, then down. Moving from 12 to 3 follows the clock hands, so it is a clockwise turn. In fact, going…

workedExamples.title: Which turn direction?
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf stands in the courtyard. The well is in front of him and Omar is on his right. Yusuf makes a half turn. Now where is the well, and where is Omar?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A half turn makes Yusuf face the opposite way. The well, which was in front, is now behind him. Omar, who was on his right, is now on his left, because turning right round swaps left and right. Worked Example 5: Which turn direction? A clock hand moves from the top (12) round to the 3 on the right. Is this clockwise or anticlockwise? Solution: Clock hands naturally move from 12 towards the 3 on the right, then down. Moving from 12 to 3 follows the clock hands, so it is a clockwise turn. In fact, going…

workedExamples.title: Counting quarter turns
workedExamples.prompt: Halima faces the masjid. She makes two quarter turns clockwise. Which way does she face now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: One quarter turn clockwise makes her face to her right. A second quarter turn (two in total) makes another right-corner turn, so she now faces away from the masjid – the opposite direction. Two quarter turns together make one half turn.

workedExamples.title: Following turn instructions
workedExamples.prompt: Farah faces forwards. She follows: quarter turn clockwise, then a half turn. Which way does she face at the end?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start facing forwards. A quarter turn clockwise makes her face to the right. Then a half turn spins her to the opposite way, so from facing right she now faces left. So Farah finishes facing to the left.

workedExamples.title: Reverse instructions
workedExamples.prompt: Idris takes 2 steps forward, then a quarter turn clockwise, then a quarter turn anticlockwise, then 2 steps back. Where does he end up, and which way does he face?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The quarter turn clockwise and the quarter turn anticlockwise cancel out, because one is the reverse of the other, so he faces the same way as at the start. The 2 steps forward and 2 steps back also cancel out. So Idris ends up exactly where he started, facing the same way. Reverse instructions always undo each other.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Complete the sentence: two quarter turns make one ___ turn, and four quarter turns make one ___ turn.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Halima is next to Sahra, and Sahra is between Halima and the door. Who is closest to the door?
workedExamples.solution: Sahra is closest to the door.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Sort these into symmetrical and not symmetrical: the letter A, the letter F, the letter T, the letter R.
workedExamples.solution: Symmetrical: A, T. Not symmetrical: F, R.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf faces the masjid. He makes three quarter turns anticlockwise. Which way does he face now? Explain.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Three quarter turns anticlockwise from the masjid: first faces left, second faces backwards, third faces right – so Yusuf faces to the right. (Three quarter turns anticlockwise equals one quarter turn clockwise.)

practice.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

activities.title: The Mirror Fold
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a small mirror if you have one.
steps: Fold a piece of paper in half and open it again so you can see the fold line.
Draw half of a simple shape (like half a heart or half a leaf) against the fold line.
Fold the paper and trace the shape through, or use the mirror to see the other half.
Open it up. You have made a symmetrical shape! The fold line is its line of symmetry.
Try making a symmetrical butterfly, flower, or masjid arch this way.

activities.title: Paint-Blob Butterflies
activities.materials: paper, and a little paint, ink, or thin mud.
steps: Fold a paper in half, then open it flat.
Drop a few blobs of colour on one side only, near the fold.
Fold the paper closed and press gently all over.
Open it up. Both sides now match – a perfect symmetrical print! Talk about why the two sides are the same.

activities.title: Symmetry Hunt
activities.materials: just your eyes and your home or neighbourhood.
steps: Walk around and look for things with a line of symmetry: leaves, doors, windows, patterns on cloth.
For each one, point to where the line of symmetry is.
Find something that is not symmetrical too, and say how you know.
Count how many symmetrical things you can find in five minutes.

activities.title: The Direction Game (Family Game)
activities.materials: your family or friends and a bit of open space.
steps: One person is the caller. The others stand and face forwards.
The caller gives instructions: quarter turn clockwise, half turn, step forwards, quarter turn anticlockwise.
Everyone follows carefully. Anyone who turns the wrong way sits down.
The last person still standing becomes the next caller.
This game makes clockwise, anticlockwise, half turns and quarter turns really stick!

activities.title: Treasure Directions Investigation
activities.materials: a small object to hide, and some open space.
steps: One person hides a small ‘treasure’ (a stone or a date).
They write or say directions to find it using forwards, left, right and turns – for example, forwards 4 steps, quarter turn clockwise, forwards 2 steps.
A friend follows the directions exactly to find the treasure.
Swap roles. Try writing the directions two different ways that both reach the treasure.

activities.title: Design a Symmetrical Tile
activities.materials: squared paper (or draw a grid) and two colours.
steps: Draw a line of symmetry down the middle of a grid.
Colour some squares on one side of the line.
Colour the matching squares on the other side, the same distance from the line.
Check with a mirror. Then try designing a tile with two lines of symmetry, like the patterns on a masjid wall.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand symmetry best? Which helped you understand turns? What was the trickiest part? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Symmetry Rule
rules.text: A shape has a line of symmetry if folding along it makes the two halves match exactly. A square has 4 lines, a rectangle has 2, a circle has too many to count. Some shapes have no line of symmetry at all.

rules.title: Reflection Rule
rules.text: A reflection is a flipped copy across a mirror line. It is the same size as the original, just turned over. Each point is the same distance from the mirror line on both sides.

rules.title: Turn Rule
rules.text: Clockwise follows the clock hands: top, right, bottom, left. Anticlockwise is the opposite way. A quarter turn faces you sideways. A half turn faces you the opposite way. Two quarter turns = one half turn. Four quarter turns = one full turn.

terms: When both halves of a shape match
The fold line where halves match
The opposite way to clock hands
Turning to face the opposite way
Left, right, above, below, in front, behind, between
commonMistakes: Count squares from the mirror line to keep it exact
Saying a rectangle has 4 lines of symmetry
A long rectangle has only 2 lines of symmetry
Picture a clock: hands go top, right, bottom, left
Half turn = opposite way; quarter turn = sideways

fluency.prompt: Yusuf faces the masjid. He makes three quarter turns anticlockwise. Which way does he face now? Explain.
fluency.answer: 1) Three quarter turns anticlockwise from the masjid: first faces left, second faces backwards, third faces right – so Yusuf faces to the right. (Three quarter turns anticlockwise equals one quarter turn clockwise.)
fluency.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: During Ramadan, Khadija folds a paper lantern pattern in half and it matches perfectly. What do we call a pattern whose halves match?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Omar guides his sister from the gate: forwards to the tree, quarter turn anticlockwise, then forwards to the door. If she turned clockwise instead, which shoulder would she have turned towards?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A carpenter in Mogadishu makes a window with two matching halves. Where is its line of symmetry likely to be?
realProblems.answer: Down the middle of the window (a vertical line of symmetry).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Down the middle of the window (a vertical line of symmetry).

realProblems.prompt: Farah faces the football goal. She makes a half turn to pass back to Liban. Which way is Liban compared to Farah before she turned?
realProblems.answer: Liban was behind Farah before she turned.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Liban was behind Farah before she turned.

realProblems.prompt: Idris stands facing the well. He makes a quarter turn clockwise, then another quarter turn clockwise, then a quarter turn anticlockwise. Which way does he face now?
realProblems.answer: Two quarter turns clockwise then one quarter turn anticlockwise leaves a net of one quarter turn clockwise, so from the well he faces to his right.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Two quarter turns clockwise then one quarter turn anticlockwise leaves a net of one quarter turn clockwise, so from the well he faces to his right.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf faces the masjid. He makes three quarter turns anticlockwise. Which way does he face now? Explain.
realProblems.answer: 1) Three quarter turns anticlockwise from the masjid: first faces left, second faces backwards, third faces right – so Yusuf faces to the right. (Three quarter turns anticlockwise equals one quarter turn clockwise.)
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Three quarter turns anticlockwise from the masjid: first faces left, second faces backwards, third faces right – so Yusuf faces to the right. (Three quarter turns anticlockwise equals one quarter turn clockwise.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Yusuf faces the masjid. He makes three quarter turns anticlockwise. Which way does he face now? Explain.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) Three quarter turns anticlockwise from the masjid: first faces left, second faces backwards, third faces right – so Yusuf faces to the right. (Three quarter turns anticlockwise equals one quarter turn clockwise.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Design a shape or pattern with exactly one vertical line of symmetry, and describe it.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Example: a symmetrical arch or heart – one half is a mirror image of the other across a vertical line.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Amina takes 2 steps forward, a quarter turn clockwise, 2 steps forward, then a quarter turn clockwise again. Describe the path she has walked.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: She walks two sides of a square: forward, turns the corner, forward again – making an L-shaped path (two edges of a square).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Is a half turn clockwise the same as a half turn anticlockwise? Explain your answer.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Yes – a half turn leaves you facing the opposite way whichever direction you turn, so clockwise and anticlockwise half turns finish the same.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Position and Direction Words.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Position words tell us exactly where something is. Direction words tell us which way something faces or moves. These words are some of the most useful in the whole of maths, because we use them to give directions and describe the world. Here are the most important ones: Left and right – the two sides. Your right hand is the one you usually write or eat with.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Turns - Clockwise and Anticlockwise.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: When something spins or turns round, it goes in one of two directions. We name these directions using a clock. Clockwise is the direction the hands of a clock move: from the top, they go to the right, then down, then to the left, then back up. Anticlockwise is the opposite direction: up, then left, then down, then right. The word anticlockwise means against the clock. Picture a clock face on the wall. The hands start at the top (12), move to the 3 on the right, down to the 6 at the bottom, across to the 9 on the…

selfAssessment: I can understand what a line of symmetry is and find it in shapes and pictures.
I can complete the other half of a symmetrical shape or pattern.
I can understand reflection and how a mirror line makes a matching image.
I can make your own symmetrical patterns using colours and shapes.
I can use position and direction words: left, right, forwards, backwards, above, below, in front of, behind, next to, between.
I can describe movement using the words clockwise and anticlockwise.
I can describe turns as a half turn or a quarter turn.
I can give and follow clear instructions to move from one place to another.
